channel.title: database on David's Blog
channel.description: Recent content in database on David's Blog

item.title: Index and Primary Key
item.description: 1. Index concepts Learn more: https://www.oreilly.com/library/view/high-performance-mysql/0596003064/ch04.html 2. When will index be created Primary Key: By default, when you define a primary key constraint on a column or set of columns using the PRIMARY KEY keyword, MySQL automatically creates an index on that column(s). 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 # index will created on user_id column create table `user` ( `user_id` smallint not null auto_increment, `username` varchar(20) not null, `password` varchar(20) not null, primary key (`user_id`) ); Unique Constraints: When you define a unique constraint on a column or set of columns using the UNIQUE keyword, MySQL automatically creates an index to enforce uniqueness.

item.title: Caching & Session Management
item.description: 1. Session Session is a server-side state management technique that allows the storage and retrieval of user-specific data across multiple requests. It provides a way to maintain state for individual users during their interaction with a web application. Here are some key points about session: Session data is stored on the server, usually in memory or in a session store. Each user is assigned a unique session ID, typically stored as a cookie or in the URL.

item.title: Caching Strategies and What should be Cached
item.description: Previous post: https://davidzhu.xyz/post/database/redis/004-session-vs-cache/ A paragraph I like, and share it here with you: “The real problem is that programmers have spent far too much time worrying about efficiency in the wrong places and at the wrong times; premature optimization is the root of all evil (or at least most of it) in programming.” - Donald Knuth Premature optimization is always a disaster. It’s not too late to add caching when actual bottlenecks are discovered after deployment or users’ feedback.

item.title: Introduce Redis - In-memory Database
item.description: 1. Redis data types Redis, an acronym for Remote Dictionary Server, is an open-source, in-memory data structure store. It can be employed as a database, cache, and even a message broker. Unlike traditional databases that read and write data to disk, Redis operates primarily in memory, which is one of the key reasons behind its lightning-fast data retrieval capabilities. 1.1. String Redis Strings is one of the most versatile of Redis’ building blocks, a binary-safe data structure, binary-safe means the strings in Redis can be any binary data.

item.title: Useful Redis Commands
item.description: 1. Install & run redis server Install: 1 $ brew install redis Run Redis in the foreground: 1 $ redis-server As an alternative to running Redis in the foreground, you can also start the process in the background: 1 2 3 $ brew services start redis $ brew services stop redis 2. Connect to redis server Once Redis is running, you can connect it by running Redis Command Line Interface - redis-cli:

item.title: ORM vs JPA vs Hibernate & JDBC vs MySQL Driver, Spring学习(三)
item.description: 1. ORM Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) is a technique that lets you query and manipulate data from a database using an object-oriented paradigm. When most people say “ORM” they are referring to the library that implements this technique. Whichever ORM library you choose, they all use the same principles. There are a lot of ORM libraries around here: Java: Hibernate. PHP: Propel or Doctrine (I prefer the

item.title: MySQL 命名规则 时间字符串类型 基础创表语句
item.description: 1. Useful commands Enter MySQL server: 1 2 3 4 $ mysql -u root -p778899 mysql> show databases; mysql> create database gptbot; mysql> use gptbot; Create table: 1 2 3 4 5 6 CREATE TABLE `user` ( `user_id` SMALLINT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT, `username` VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, `password` VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, PRIMARY KEY (`user_id`) ); 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 show tables; show columns from user; ALTER TABLE
